thread.subject: The joy of combos and the pain of downtime

article.subject: The joy of combos and the pain of downtime
article.body:
Terraforming Mars is a card synergy engine that happens to have a board game attached to it. I mean that as a compliment, mostly. The best moments are when you spot a combo across three or four cards that nobody else sees, and you spend the next few rounds building it up while pretending you're just doing normal things. The card variety is enormous, and even after many plays there are still combinations I haven't tried.

The production quality is famously rough around the edges, but honestly, once you're deep in a game you stop noticing. What bothers me more is game length at higher player counts. At two or three players it moves well. At four or five it drags, because you're waiting a long time between turns and the engine building rewards you for optimizing your own tableau more than interacting with others. It can feel like parallel solitaire in the late game.

I keep coming back to it though, which says something. The draft variant helps a lot with reducing randomness. And there's a reason the game has stayed near the top of BGG for years. The core mechanism of converting resources into terraforming progress while building an engine of card combos is just satisfying in a way that's hard to replicate.

article.subject: Re: The joy of combos and the pain of downtime
article.body: Best engine builder I have played so far, but needs some deluxe components to claim that title. I always play it as 3-Player game and don't want to play it with more. I really dislike what the expansions are doing, none of them adds something that makes the game more enjoyable. Except for Prelude of course, haven't played without it since it was released. Venus just adds another track and gamelength; I would never play with world government rules as solution. Colonies is atleast thematically appealling, but the gameplay-additions are lackluster. Turmoil with its mini-parliament and global events is actually quite interesting. But the events somehow didn't really click for me in practice and in the end there ain't enough project-cards that interact with the parliament. Atleast when your deck is swamped with promos and fanmade expansions like mine. Also, who wants to add another 30min on an already long game? I just took the cards from venus and colonies that work with the base game and havent pulled any of those expansions for a very long time. The 3D Tiles help giving the "attached" boardgame a little more attention.

article.subject: Re: The joy of combos and the pain of downtime
article.body:
Wiesl wrote:

Best engine builder I have played so far, but needs some deluxe components to claim that title. I always play it as 3-Player game and don't want to play it with more. I really dislike what the expansions are doing, none of them adds something that makes the game more enjoyable. Except for Prelude of course, haven't played without it since it was released. Venus just adds another track and gamelength; I would never play with world government rules as solution. Colonies is atleast thematically appealling, but the gameplay-additions are lackluster. Turmoil with its mini-parliament and global events is actually quite interesting. But the events somehow didn't really click for me in practice and in the end there ain't enough project-cards that interact with the parliament. Atleast when your deck is swamped with promos and fanmade expansions like mine. Also, who wants to add another 30min on an already long game? I just took the cards from venus and colonies that work with the base game and havent pulled any of those expansions for a very long time. The 3D Tiles help giving the "attached" boardgame a little more attention.

Venus should not be adding much game length, as the 1st player is supposed to be moving a terraforming indicator at the end of the turn. Turmoil is the biggest offender for adding length to the game.

article.subject: Re: The joy of combos and the pain of downtime
article.body:
mcpranno wrote:


Venus should not be adding much game length, as the 1st player is supposed to be moving a terraforming indicator at the end of the turn. Turmoil is the biggest offender for adding length to the game.
 Yes, I referred to the world-government-rules. It is a very artificial way to speed up the game. If it was a good solution you could just say that with turmoil you move the indicator two steps every time. But it isn't a good solution. I don't like it and won't play with it. If I want to speed up the game, I would rather hand everyone 5 Prelude cards of which they can keep 3.

article.subject: Re: The joy of combos and the pain of downtime
article.body:
Prelude is an absolute must and player count should never be higher than 3!
Everything else can be ignored unless you're a sucker for punishment and a slave to AP

article.subject: Re: The joy of combos and the pain of downtime
article.body: I like the game and prefer it with no expansions. I don't mind Prelude, but my general preference is without it.

article.subject: Re: The joy of combos and the pain of downtime
article.body:
Parkersburg wrote:

I like the game and prefer it with no expansions. I don't mind Prelude, but my general preference is without it.

I agree but I do like Prelude and I love the map expansions.

article.subject: Re: The joy of combos and the pain of downtime
article.body: i actually always say the opposite: terraforming mars is a fight for award and board spaces with a decent engine builder attached. i found after 10 or so base game plays that you get repeat playpatterns and things you will pick/ignore, e.g. you can have a pretty solid card ranking that only somewhat changes based on circumstance. like, for my money, the card play is fine, the drafting is decent but the real crux is the timing based plays on awards and terraforming steps, especially when you make room for which standard projects.

article.subject: Re: The joy of combos and the pain of downtime
article.body:
The OP scans as AI text.

So many fake reviews these days.
